You may also find it helpful to educate internally, or even QA work to see if any step could have downfalls..."},{"url":"https://cognition.com/blog/devin-can-now-schedule-devins","title":"Devin can now Schedule Devins | Cognition","content":"Every engineering team has a quiet backlog of things that should happen on schedule, but often slip – like weekly release notes, feature flag cleanups, and onboarding QA sweeps.
Starting today, Devin can schedule its own sessions to take care of work like this.
The most natural way into this feature is to let Devin do something once and then tell it to keep doing it.
Now, you can simply tell Devin: "Schedule this for every Monday at 9am." Devin will set up a schedule to wake up each Monday at 9am and run the same workflow, so you can stop thinking about it.
Here are a few examples of the kinds of recurring work that Scheduled Devins handle well."},{"url":"https://www.linkedin.com/posts/rowancheung_cognition-ai-just-introduced-devin-an-autonomous-activity-7173351188933799936-fMo5","title":"Cognition AI just introduced Devin, an autonomous coding agent that not only autocompletes tasks but can also write entire apps in minutes. And even its own browser."},{"url":"https://www.augmentcode.com/tools/intent-vs-devin","title":"Devin vs Intent (2026): Autonomous Agent or Developer-in-the-Loop Platform? | Augment Code","content":"Devin completes its scoped task correctly and can use repo-wide search, planning, and tests to understand dependencies beyond the files it edits, but it does not enforce cross-service consistency through a shared living spec the way Intent does.
A 2.25-hour task consumes roughly 9 ACUs ($18-$20.25 depending on plan tier).
Augment Code assumes continuous developer interaction with shared team resources.
Cognition states the acquisition includes Windsurf's IP and product codebase.
The opportunity: Cognition is building an integrated IDE-plus-agent platform that could deliver on Devin's autonomous capabilities with Windsurf's developer-facing IDE experience.
Augment Code’s Context Engine already integrates with MCP tools, including Cursor, Claude Code, and Zed, through MCP compatibility.
Intent assumes the developer is the architect and agents are the builders.
The choice between Intent and Devin comes down to how your team works: structured multi-agent coordination with architectural oversight, or autonomous task delegation for well-scoped, isolated work."}]}

A lot of tools can help you write code, but fewer tools can wake up when something breaks and start investigating the problem. Watch the video below: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IkUBk7Fp02o Want to make money and save time with AI? Get AI Coaching, Support & Courses 👉 https://www.skool.com/ai-profit-lab-7462/about New Devin AI Update Feels Like A Real Workflow Shift New Devin AI Update matters because it changes where the AI agent enters the work. Most AI coding tools wait until you ask for help. You open the project, explain the issue, paste the context, and then ask the tool to do something useful. That can help, but it still leaves the human doing the first messy part of the job. Devin is moving into that messy first part. When a bug, alert, or ticket appears, Devin can start investigating before someone manually assigns the task. That is why this update feels different. The agent is not just helping after the work begins. It can become part of the workflow that starts the work. That is a much bigger deal than another code generation feature. Auto Triage Is The Main Reason Devin AI Update Matters Auto triage is the feature that makes the New Devin AI Update feel practical. When a bug ticket appears, Devin can read the issue, search the codebase, check recent changes, inspect logs, and prepare a summary. That is the kind of task that wastes a lot of team time. It is not always difficult, but it is repetitive. Someone has to figure out what broke. Someone has to check what changed. Someone has to hunt through logs and trace the issue back to the right file. Devin can now do much of that first pass automatically. The human still reviews the result, but the blank page is gone. That is where the time saving starts. A team can move from confusion to review much faster. New Devin AI Update Turns Context Into Leverage New Devin AI Update works because Devin is becoming more connected to the systems around the work. A bug does not usually live in one place. The ticket gives one piece of the story. The logs give another piece. The recent commits give another clue. The codebase shows where the issue might actually be fixed. A human developer usually has to pull all of that together before making a decision. Devin can now start doing that context-gathering work. That matters because context is what makes AI agents useful. Without context, an agent guesses. With context, it can investigate. The difference is massive. This is why connected agents are more important than simple chat prompts. A prompt can only go so far when the agent cannot see the systems where the work is happening. Devin AI Update Makes Memory A Serious Advantage New Devin AI Update becomes more interesting when memory enters the workflow. One of the biggest problems with AI agents has always been that they forget too much. You explain the repo once. Then you explain it again. You explain the team’s patterns once. Then the next session feels like starting from zero again. That gets tiring fast. A real teammate does not work like that. A real teammate remembers which files usually matter, which workflows are common, and which fixes worked before. Devin’s memory moves the agent closer to that kind of relationship. The more it works inside a clear system, the more useful it can become. That is a big shift. It means the agent is not only completing tasks. It can also learn from past sessions and improve the way it handles future work. The Devin AI Update Removes Repetitive Engineering Drag New Devin AI Update is not really about replacing engineers. That framing misses the point. The better way to think about Devin is that it can remove repetitive engineering drag. Bug triage is drag. Dependency updates are drag. Failed build checks are drag. Incident summaries are drag. Daily error scanning is drag. These jobs are important, but they are not usually where the best thinking happens. They follow patterns. They require patience, context, and consistency. That is where AI agents can help. Devin can handle the first pass, then pass the work back to a human for review. The human keeps control. The agent removes the repetitive digging. That is a practical division of work. New Devin AI Update Rewards Clear Team Playbooks New Devin AI Update also proves that AI agents are only as useful as the workflow around them. If your process is unclear, the agent will struggle. If your process is documented, the agent has a better chance of doing useful work. That is why playbooks matter. A good playbook tells Devin what should trigger the workflow, where to look, what to check, what output to create, and when a human should step in. This does not need to be complicated. It just needs to be clear. Most teams keep too much process knowledge inside people’s heads. That works until the team gets busy. Then everyone wastes time rediscovering the same information. AI agents force teams to write down the work properly. Inside the AI Profit Boardroom , this is the type of agent workflow thinking that matters most because real automation starts with repeatable systems. New Devin AI Update Is Bigger Than Coding New Devin AI Update is a coding story, but the pattern is bigger than coding. A task appears. An agent gathers context. The agent checks the right systems. Then it prepares the next step for review. That pattern applies to more than software bugs. It can apply to support tickets. It can apply to new leads. It can apply to onboarding. It can apply to weekly reporting. It can apply to inbox triage. It can apply to customer follow-up. The specific tool may change, but the structure stays the same. Every business has repeatable work that quietly drains time. The smart move is to find those tasks and turn them into workflows. Devin is showing this pattern clearly in engineering first. The same idea will spread across the rest of business operations. Devin AI Update Still Needs Human Judgment New Devin AI Update is powerful, but it still needs human review. That is not a weakness. That is how serious workflows should work. An agent can investigate a bug. An agent can summarize the likely root cause. An agent can suggest a fix. An agent can even prepare a pull request. But a human still needs to check the work. Complex decisions need experience. Security changes need caution. Architecture changes need long-term thinking. Customer-facing fixes need judgment. The best setup is not blind automation. The best setup is controlled automation. Devin does the repetitive first pass, and the team reviews the important parts. That is how you get speed without losing control. Using The New Devin AI Update Without Overbuilding New Devin AI Update becomes easier to use when you start small. A lot of teams make automation harder than it needs to be. They try to automate everything at once. That usually creates confusion. A better approach is to pick one repeatable workflow. Choose one repo. Pick one common issue. Write down the steps your team already follows. Then turn those steps into a simple Devin playbook. Tell the agent what to read first, which logs matter, what recent changes to check, and what kind of summary the team needs. That gives Devin a narrow job. Narrow jobs are easier to test. They are easier to review. They are easier to improve. Once one workflow works, you can expand from there. That is how practical agent adoption happens. New Devin AI Update Points To Smaller Faster Teams New Devin AI Update points toward a world where small teams can move faster with the right agent stack. This does not mean people stop mattering. It means people can focus on better work. Instead of spending hours digging through logs, they can review a prepared summary. Instead of manually checking the same failures, they can improve the system. Instead of writing every incident report from scratch, they can edit a draft. That changes the pace of work. A small team with clear playbooks and useful agents can move faster than a larger team doing everything manually. That is the part business owners should notice. The advantage will not only come from having more people. It will come from having better workflows. The teams that learn this early will have a serious edge. The New Devin AI Update Shows Where Agents Are Going New Devin AI Update is a signal that AI agents are moving into daily operations. They are not only answering questions. They are responding to triggers. They are reading tools. They are checking context. They are remembering past work. They are preparing outputs for humans to review. That is where AI agents become useful. The future is not just a bigger chat window. The future is connected workflows where agents handle the first pass of repeatable work. Devin is one example of this shift. The bigger lesson is that clear workflows are becoming a business advantage. Teams that document their work, connect their tools, and review agent output properly will move faster. For step-by-step help building AI agent workflows, the AI Profit Boardroom is where you can learn how to turn updates like this into practical business systems. Frequently Asked Questions About New Devin AI Update What is the New Devin AI Update? The New Devin AI Update adds stronger agent workflows, including auto triage, connected tools, memory, and the ability to investigate repeatable technical issues with less manual prompting. What makes the Devin AI Update useful? The update is useful because Devin can start the first pass of work by reading tickets, checking logs, searching code, and preparing context for human review. Can Devin replace developers? Devin should not be treated as a full replacement for developers because complex features, architecture, security decisions, and final approval still need human judgment. What tasks are best for Devin? Devin is best for repeatable engineering work such as bug triage, small fixes, dependency updates, failed build checks, incident summaries, and daily error reviews. Why should business owners care about Devin AI? Business owners should care because the same agent workflow pattern can help with leads, support tickets, onboarding, reports, inbox triage, and other repeatable tasks.
